eras.name: Colonial & Frontier Era
eras.description: A period defined by practical, rugged attire for survival and westward expansion. Homespun fabrics and buckskin were common among settlers, while urban elites mimicked simplified European styles.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Historical illustration of an American frontiersman in a fringed buckskin jacket and a woman in a simple blue homespun cotton dress with a white apron and mob cap, 1820s wilderness setting.

eras.name: The Gilded Age
eras.description: An era of extreme wealth and industrial growth, characterized by the 'S-bend' silhouette and the rise of the Gibson Girl. High fashion was dictated by social status and elaborate dress codes for different times of day.
eras.aiImagePrompt: High-society New York ballroom scene in 1890, women in silk bustle gowns with intricate lace detailing and men in formal black tailcoats and white waistcoats.

eras.name: The Jazz Age & Flapper Era
eras.description: Post-war rebellion led to the abandonment of the corset in favor of straight, loose silhouettes. This era saw the birth of the 'modern woman' and the integration of sportswear into daily life.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Art Deco style depiction of a flapper woman in a beaded chemise dress with fringe and a cloche hat, standing next to a man in a wide-legged suit, 1920s jazz club background.

eras.name: Mid-Century Americana
eras.description: The rise of the American middle class and the 'New Look' influence. Fashion became gender-rigid but mass-produced, with the emergence of teenage culture and rock-and-roll style.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1950s American diner scene, a girl in a pink poodle skirt and saddle shoes, a boy in a black leather motorcycle jacket and cuffed blue jeans.

eras.name: Disco & The Designer Denim Revolution
eras.description: The transition from hippie counterculture to high-glam disco and the birth of designer branding. Jeans moved from workwear to high-fashion status symbols through brands like Calvin Klein.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Studio 54 aesthetic, a woman in a jersey wrap dress and platform heels, a man in a wide-collared polyester leisure suit under disco lights.

eras.name: Grunge & 90s Minimalism
eras.description: A rejection of 80s excess, characterized by Seattle-born grunge and New York's sleek, monochromatic minimalism. Streetwear began its ascent into the mainstream.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1990s urban street style, a model in a silk slip dress over a white t-shirt, another person in an oversized plaid flannel shirt and ripped denim.

eras.name: Athleisure & The Digital Frontier
eras.description: The total blurring of lines between gym wear, street style, and luxury. Fashion is driven by social media trends, hype-culture, and a growing emphasis on inclusivity and tech-wear.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Modern New York street scene, an influencer wearing an oversized designer hoodie, cycling shorts, chunky luxury sneakers, and a small crossbody bag.

textiles.name: Denim
textiles.description: A sturdy cotton warp-faced textile in which the weft passes under two or more warp threads.
textiles.technique: Indigo dyeing and twill weaving with copper rivet reinforcement.

textiles.name: Sea Island Cotton
textiles.description: An extra-long staple cotton known for its silk-like texture and high durability.

textiles.name: Gore-Tex
textiles.description: A waterproof, breathable fabric membrane used extensively in American outdoor and techwear.

textiles.name: American Fleece
textiles.description: A soft, thick fabric with a napped surface, synonymous with the American sweatshirt.
textiles.technique: Circular knitting followed by mechanical brushing.

climate.fashionImplication: The vast regional climate differences necessitate a 'layering culture.' Heavy wools and technical outerwear dominate the Northeast and Midwest, while lightweight cottons and performance synthetics are essential in the South and West.

culturalInfluences: Hollywood and Celebrity Red Carpet Culture
Hip-Hop and African American Urban Style

notableEvents: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival
